items.title: IASB Issues IFRS 20 for Rate-Regulated Entities
items.summary: The IASB has issued IFRS 20, a new standard requiring companies subject to rate regulation - think utilities like electricity, water and gas providers - to recognise regulatory assets and liabilities on their balance sheets. It's designed to give investors a clearer picture of how rate regulation shapes future cash flows, and takes effect for annual periods beginning on or after 1 January 2029.

items.title: FRC Revises UK Auditing Standards on Reporting
items.summary: The FRC has revised three UK auditing standards (ISA (UK) 700, 701 and 720) following public consultation, aiming to make auditors' reports shorter, clearer and more useful to investors. The changes, which also clarify auditor responsibilities under the revised UK Corporate Governance Code, take effect from 15 December 2026.

items.title: FRC Modernises Audit Enforcement Procedure
items.summary: The FRC has overhauled its Audit Enforcement Procedure as part of a broader shift to a more integrated regulatory model, linking supervision, investigation and enforcement so problems are spotted earlier and resolved faster. The reform is intended to give the audit market quicker outcomes and earlier lessons learned from enforcement cases.

items.title: PCAOB Proposes Easing Quality Control Standard
items.summary: The PCAOB has proposed amendments to its QC 1000 quality control standard that would remove the external quality control function and design-only compliance requirements, responding to pushback from audit firms over implementation burden. The proposal is open for public comment until 9 July 2026.

items.title: PCAOB Forms Inspections Modernization Council
items.summary: The PCAOB is setting up an Inspections Modernization Council and invited outside experts to apply, signalling an effort to rethink how it inspects audit firms. The application window closed mid-June 2026, with the council expected to feed into future changes to the inspections programme.

items.title: Big Four Race to Build AI Assurance Services
items.summary: PwC, EY, Deloitte and KPMG are racing to build out AI assurance services that audit the safety, fairness and effectiveness of clients' own AI systems, alongside KPMG's $2bn AI investment programme and EY's rollout of AI agents across its tax practice. The push reflects how the Big Four are repositioning audit and advisory work around AI risk, not just adopting AI internally.
